sentences.text: Yes.
Approximately.
Yes.
Yes, and I still went to the cheder over there, and my-- and we still did the normal, like the-- my mother and my sister were attending at home to doing the cooking and everything.
I went to the cheder, and my father used to do a bit of business in Wieliczka and a bit of business at Cracow, coming there and back.
And then, after, for some reason, we had to go back to-- to Cracow, to the ghetto.
All the Jews had to go to ghetto, and we went, too.
I think so.
But maybe we did not wait too long, because when we came to the, to Cracow, we had quite a good accommodation.
We, we managed to get a, a good accommodation in Cracow.
It was not more than two, two rooms, but it was sufficient for us.
And we stayed in Cracow-- well, also, in the ghetto, we're also comfortable.
There was no problem, as far as food or something else.
I think I put it-- it could be, it could be about six or eight months before the liquidation.
The liquidation was in March, '43, correct?
We were there before, and when did Emalia start?
The real Emalia?
Well, I can't remember it, because it would be about six, eight, nine months ago when I first start to go to Emalia with the first 60 people.
Could be.
Could be '42.
And that was the whole '42.
Not quite '42.
Not quite, but let's say until October '42.
In 1942, I start with Emalia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by that time, my father and my mother and my sister and her child were taken away.
It, it, it wasn't-- they didn't wait until the liquidation of the ghetto.
They were on the list.
The Kapo came, and they were on the list, and they had to go.
I wasn't on the list, because I was young, and my brother wasn't on the list.
And, and my sister was not on the list, too, but she, but she didn't want to leave my parents, and she didn't want-- she was worried about the child.
The child was only about four years or something like that.
So she went together.
Yeah.

Well, we used to buy food, and I used to go from time to time, being the youngest, used to go to-- because it was across the road-- to the neighbors, and I used to buy from them or get from them the food.
I was caught once.
That, I remembered very well.
I was caught once by a Polish policeman.
I had bread with me, a whole bread, and I was caught by the Polish policeman.
And he said to me, you're a Jew.
Because I didn't wear the-- because I, I couldn't be-- so I said, I'm not a Jew.
So he said, come to the-- come, come into the-- you know, they used to have a big-- before you went into the block of flats, there was a, a big door.
So he said come behind the door and take off your trousers.
So I didn't went so far.
I said to him, I am a Jew, and I start to cry, and I tell him, look, I only came here because we haven't got much bread, and I want to go back.
So he said to me, I'll let you out this time.
And this was the truth.
He let me out.
But if I ever catch you again, you're gone.
And that was-- and I never went, since then, because I was scared that he would be there, and he would recognize me.
It was the only accident which I will never forget.
How old were you with this incident?
I was about 15, maybe, 15 or 15 and 1/2, yeah.
It was illegal to get in.
It was illegal to smuggle the food into the ghetto.
But, you know, I did a few times, and once I was caught, and that's it.
And I wasn't caught by the German.
I was caught by the Polish policeman.
But because-- I believe, because I didn't went as far as took off my trousers, because if I would have took my trousers, they knew that I was circumcised.
Because the Polish people were not circumcised before the war.
So I didn't went this far.
I said, look, I am a Jew.
And I told him the whole story, and I plead with him, and so he, he let me out.
He let me have the bread, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the only story which I didn't tell you, but when we came to it, I must tell you.
Yeah.
There was a kitchen where they used to get-- everybody used to get soup.
The soup was quite [INAUDIBLE].
But besides, there was some shops in ghetto.
You could obtain food and other things.
Yes, so a few businesses were--
Legally, yes, because it was in the ghetto.
They smuggled, probably.
They had some of it, you know.
I wouldn't know.
I wasn't, I wasn't that kind of-- but I know one thing, that you could get it.
You had to have money.
With Polish Zloty.
Proper Polish currency, yes.
And this currency could be converted from gold, from gold.
You could convert into that currency from jewelry, you could convert.
The people who buy gold, who buy the jewelry, who buy diamonds, you know?
You could convert it.
There was a value for it.
Yes.
We had, we had, we had plenty.
As a matter of fact, I don't know whether I should say on television, but I still got hidden jewelry in Poland.
And I went to the Polish, to the Polish consul here, and I asked them, I asked them whether I could go, if they assist me to take it.
And so he say to me that, if it is in the ground, it is the property of the government.
I said, it's not in the ground.
It's in the wall.
He said to me, how far is from the ground?
So I could see that I would have difficulty.
So I leave it there.
I know where it is, and it is still there.
I don't know what the value today, but it would be gold, jewelery, diamonds, and, and, and what you call it-- and a golden watch, which we convert it all.
Because we had it just [INAUDIBLE] a little bit before the German came.
Yeah.
They were taken away, they were taken just about a month before Emalia.
Let's say about August, August or July in 1942.
No. '43 was finished.
Yes.
They, they came with a list, the, the Jewish Kapo.
You know, they were Jewish militia, actually.
They were dressed like a policeman, Jewish policeman.
They had a star on the, on the cap.
As they came with the list, they said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], we have to take you away.
Early in the morning.
Very early in the morning.
It could be before the dawn.
And the whole ghetto was surrounded by the, by, by the German soldiers.
Whenever there was action like that, there was fence that you couldn't escape.
After they took a certain amount of people, there was no more guards.
Just before.
This would be about six or nine months liquidation.
Because they couldn't liquidate the whole ghetto at one go, so they liquidated gradually.
Yes.
No.
Inside, there was no Jewish-- the German soldiers.
But they, they had to go to a certain point, you know, to a-- what you call it-- where they all worked together.
And they were sent by, by, by the trucks to the station, and to [INAUDIBLE].
It was, it was-- well, I don't know.
They had a certain quota, but I wouldn't know how much it is.
And also, a lot of people knew before that, because they had contact with the militia.
Because militia got, got all this about the night before.
And if they contact their parents and say, these people were safe, because they hide themselves.
We didn't have that connection.
We didn't have such a good connection.
If we would have such a connection, probably my father would go underground, or somewhere.
Yes, there were quite a few actions.
But I only experience one, because that's where I experience when that happened to my parents.
That's right, yes.
Yes, there was also other action, but at that particular time, we were not on the list.
I heard it.
I heard it, but I didn't see it.
Could be.
Could be, yes.
But it wasn't a month before the liquidation.
It was--
No, no.
I wasn't there when the liquidation was there, because I went to Emalia, as I told you, and we used to go from, from the ghetto to Emalia.
We used to walk.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah.
The Schindler's factory.
Yes.
By coincidence.
I walk on the street, and I, I met a friend, and, and, and there was people standing, not in a queue, but in two rows.
And there was a German over there, and there was a, a fellow which I knew, Bankier, which he was there.
So I ask him, what's going on here?
He said to me, they want plumbers for Emalia factory.
He said it's supposed to be a good position, because you work inside.
And I said, but you're not a plumber.
I said, all right, I will try it.
So I went, and I, I joined the queue, joined the row.
And when I joined the row, when the German came to me, he said to me, are you a plumber?
I said, look, my parents are plumbers, you know.
I come from a family of plumbers.
So he took a risk, and he put me on that side where, where the 60 people is, and that's it.
And I went to the factory.
And the, and the next day-- I can't remember whether it was next day or the same day-- I went to the Emalia factory, and they put me-- I was, I was practically the youngest from the whole lot.
And, and, and they put me in charge of a Polish foreman.
So the Polish foreman came to me, what can you do?
I said, I can't do anything, I just want to come here, because I heard it would help me.
So he said, all right, you, you have, you have a job.
Here, take the broom and sweep all the floor.
And that's how I started.
And he taught me slowly how to, how to-- because it wasn't-- you didn't have to be skillful.
All you had to do is to put a piece of metal under this special machine, and the machine went down, and the part come out.
So it wasn't a job which you had to learn, go to the university.
You could learn in five minutes.
And that's how I entered Emalia.
And I was there-- after that, I went to work outside.
And then after, at the end of the Emalia, I used to work where I attend where there was the heating system for the whole factory.
I had to pull cord, and I was to work at nighttime.
This was the job.
This was my job in Emalia.
Bankier.
Bankier was the old man who, who, who was-- he was the owner, before the war, of Emalia factory.
No, it was Emalia.
It was-- before the war, the Polish name Emalia.
Emalia.
E-M-L-I-A.
L-I-A.
Why, why it was called Emalia, because they used to make pots, like Emalia, you know.
They put them in the oven, and they used to be--
Enamel.
That's right.
That's how it was.
So they called Emalia.
And he went bankrupt before the war, with his other two partners, and Oskar Schindler took over the factory, but he needed somebody who knew something of the business part.
Bankier was the one who looked after it.
Stern, I never come in touch with Stern, but I knew Bankier.
And Bankier--
Stern which is in the Oskar-- the The accountant.
He might be dead, but I can't remember it.
I only remember Bankier.
Abraham Bankier, I think.
[INAUDIBLE]
Abraham Bankier?
Or is it different on the list?
We'll have to look at the list, yes.
He was the most important man for Schindler.
How do I know?
Because he's the one who did, who did direct him to get the factory, and he's the one who run for him the factory, from the office.
Because he knew the business.
He knew the Emalia business.
But--
Schindler didn't know anything about pots and pans.
Of course.
He knew it, because this factory was established before the war.
That's right.
Never.
Always-- as soon as we came to Emalia, to Emalia, when we enter it, we went to different ways, and he went to the office.
I never saw him until the evening, when we came back.
He wasn't involved any physical work.
He eat and drink-- he didn't eat with us.
Nothing.
He probably had special food, and all these things.
And, and he was the one who, who, ran the Emalia.
The most important person.
That's right.
That's right.
He was.
I know him from before the war, because he used to live in that, in that place in [NON-ENGLISH] where my sister was living, in the same building.
I knew him before, and he knew me too.
But-- and, and when I went to Emalia, he didn't, he didn't want to make [INAUDIBLE] that he knows me, or that there's some, some connection, but, but he didn't do me any harm.
He helped me in many ways, like, for example, I don't know whether he wasn't the one who, who did try to get me on the list, or something, when later came, you know.
So indirectly, you know.
I have no idea, but I know one thing, that I knew him.
All I-- when I saw him, I said good morning and good evening, and that's it.
Nobody was important except him, but I knew a lot of people who were there working on the factory.
He was the only one who worked in the office.
Pemper.
Pemper was in, in ghetto.
He wasn't in Emalia from the beginning.
He came after, when the Schindler list was made.
No, no.
Pemper, I know from before the war, because Pemper's brother used to go with me to school, to the Polish school, and he could not-- he was very bad in geography.
And when I was sitting in the first row, the teacher called him, show me where France is.
So he looked up in, in where Scandinavia is.
So I, I said to him, down, down, down, and [LAUGHS] eventually he caught France.
He saw on there written, France, and he caught it, and we used to be good friends, you know.
That sort of thing.
And through that, he put a good word to his brother when the list was made in the ghetto.
Mietek.
Yes.
Mietek.
I don't know, was it Mietek, or-- I can't remember.
One of them was [?
Ignatz ?] Pemper, one was Mietek Pemper.
Yes.
Because when, when I was in Plaszow, when I knew that it was going to-- they need 1,000 people for the, for the list.
So I said, put a good word for me.
He said that I should make sure that I'd been on list.
And I didn't even rely on him.
I went direct to his brother, and he knew me, too, and he said to me, I will be on the list.
His brother was the secretary of Amon Goeth.
Amon Goeth?
He was the commander of the Plaszow concentration camp.
Yes.
He was the most important person.
Bankier was the most important.
There were quite a few important persons there.
Yes, that's right.
No, no.
He had him because, because he-- Pemper, he, he was a very skilled man.
I mean, nobody could do what-- he could talk to you, like you do, and at the same time writing on the machine, and, and no make mistakes.
Yes.
He could have done it.
But there could be there were some monkey business behind it, which we never know.
Yes.
I was in Emalia until the time where I had to go to Plaszow.
Until, I think, the end of '44.
Not quite end of '44.
Well, I was there-- as soon as I went to Emalia-- first, we went from-- as you know-- from Emalia to, to ghetto every day.
Then after, before the liquidation of the ghetto, we went from, from Plaszow to Emalia.
I believe that was-- I was taken before the liquidation.
Could be-- could be something like that.
Yeah.
Could be, yes.

In Plaszow, I can't remember how long, but we-- but as soon, but as soon as Schindler saw that it's going to be difficult to bring us, we saw all the atrocities, and we didn't know what's going to happen.
And he managed to build the barracks on Emalia, and then we came to Emalia, and we stay on Emalia.
And that could be one and a half year.
Not very long.
Could be, maybe, three months.
But it doesn't take long to build wooden barracks.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's right.
Behind the factory.
Correct.
They were still surrounded by the, by the Lithuanian guards, which they change every so often, from Plaszow.
But they had no right, ever, to come interfere with us in, in the factory.
They couldn't come in.
It was arranged through, through Schindler.
Schindler makes that arrangement.
Because we knew it.
He didn't, he did not allow anybody to interfere.
The same was in Brunnlitz.
He did not let interfere with any of [INAUDIBLE] in his factory.
There were-- the German used to come to make, sometime, inspection from Plaszow, which is the hierarchy of Germans.
Like they were, [INAUDIBLE] maybe, officer, everything.
But he used to take them upstairs to make, to make them drunk before they went down.
He used to offer some cognac and everything, so that they went back and they say, everything is OK.
But we were protected.
We didn't work under the German guards.
We were not-- we had no contact with the German guards.
Nothing at all.
Yes.
No, no.
That, I don't-- that, I don't know.
He's, he's-- I couldn't pick his brain, but I know one thing, that he did not employ any more Polish workers, which he had to pay them, and he employed the Jews.
And as the factory grew, he took more Jews from, from Plaszow.
That's what I remember, because sometimes used to come new people.
And as far-- I don't think it was benevolent, because he made money out of it.
So it was purely commercial, at that start.
Purely commercial.
He didn't-- he got paid for us, but he didn't give us any money.
He just feed us, you know.
Feed us, and he kept us in comfort.
I should say that he did-- that he was entitled to that.
My opinion is, he's entitled to that money, because he, he protect us.
Put it this way.
Where everybody was starving and, and, and, and dying in Plaszow, we were sitting over there, and, and we live like, like nothing, like a [INAUDIBLE].
He was able-- He was able to make himself money on the side, and he used to come-- there was strict guidelines.
The factory had to go.
He was working seven days a week, day and night, working day and night, or shift, and, and he was producing the pots and pans for the military and for the other things, and he was living very well.
He used to come with a-- with a Mercedes car every day dressed in different suit, and sometimes three times a day.
And he had girlfriends, you know.
He had a good life.
But we didn't have a bad life, either, because of that.
Maybe he change it after he saw what's happening in the, in the Cracow ghetto, and, and maybe-- he was a very shrewd man-- and maybe he saw as a ticket to live after the war, because he knew it.
Because that was the time when Stalingrad fell, and we got the news.
Stalingrad fell, and Odessa fell.
So he knew that the German are going bad, so he want to create a good will.
So that's what he did.
This is my opinion.
You're saying there might have been some ulterior motives?
Ulterior motives, but at the same time, he, he was not bad, and he had-- and he was always-- and he, he was looking after Bankier.
I still say Bankier was the most important man for him.
Yeah
It was horrible.
I mean, I, I wasn't there daytime, so I did not experience all this.
But each time we came at night, we had this happen, that happen.
Somebody was killed.
Somebody was hanged.
And, and you hear all the time the same thing, all the atrocities.
So we, we did-- it was not very pleasant.
We're lucky that we came alive.
We're lucky that we come out alive.
And that was-- that's where Schindler probably made a, made a, a-- what you call it-- commitment to build, to build this place on the-- At the factory.
At the factory.
And also, at the same thing-- because Bankier was coming with us, too.
He didn't know what could happen to him.
Yes.
Yeah.
In factory.
Yes.
Not to my memory.
Is that what happened?
No.
Not to my-- maybe when I was in Plaszow, but we were never in Plaszow-- I was never in Plaszow at daytime.
I was always working.
Daytime, I was always working at Emalia.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
I don't recall that.
I was all the time in Emalia.
I don't recall that.
Never had any day off.
But it wasn't so bad, because we didn't work-- I didn't work so hard.
No.
I can't remember it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only time which I remember the factory was maybe closed, and we were in Plaszow, at the time where he had to transfer all the machinery from Plaszow into Brunnlitz.
At that time, we were staying in the, in the Plaszow.
We're not doing very much over there, and, and that's what-- the Schindler List was born there.
And that's what was-- we knew that he come one day, and he will make a list and take his people to the Brunnlitz, the people to Brunnlitz.
Yes.
A few times.
Yes.
Well, he caught me one day sleeping.
It was the same, the same as with this policeman.
He caught me one day sleeping on the job, and so he also, he warned me that, if he catch me again, I'll lose the job.
That's all he did.
He didn't, he didn't, he did not do me anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He was a tall fellow, not bad looking.
I would say he would be in his 50s, 40, 45.
He wouldn't be more.
All the time smoking, always cigarette in his hand.
He was a chain smoker.
Nicely dressed, with perfume all over his place, and that's it.
That's how he was.
Well, he used to-- when he went on the inspection, he always talk to the Polish foreman.
He always talk to the Polish foreman.
And then, when, when they work at nighttime, they had an idea.
As soon as the car drive, drove-- because the, the gate wasn't very far from the main factory.
The factory was on the right-hand side, and the gate was out.
So as soon as they hear gate, they knew Schindler's coming, because they start to, you know, to make, make themselves busy, you know.
Yes, yes.
The Poles were there before we came.
No.
Only the Poles.
They were quite nice people.
They were not, they were not bad.
The foreman, as long as you did the job, they knew that we are here because of this business.
So they treat us-- as long as you have, you have, you have to do the job.
And if, if you did the job, they didn't do you any harm.
No.
They still went-- the Poles always went home.
And I don't know whether they work on Saturday.
I can't remember it, but I, I don't think they worked seven days a week.
Specific incident.
The one specific incident is one of, of the people who were stupid, a fellow from, from Emalia, he run away.
Because, from Emalia, you could easily run away.
There was no problem to, to disappear, but nobody wanted.
Because where would you go?
You haven't got where to go.
And he run away, and Schindler had quite a bit of trouble to explain it.
And the German came, and they come to the gate, and they give us another warning that if something happens, they will decimate us.
You know what decimating is?
Every 10th is going to be killed.
So we had to be careful and make sure that nobody runs away.
It was the only incident.
But otherwise, nobody in their right mind would run away from Emalia.
Because we knew what was going outside.
We, we had papers.
We had news, and the Poles used to come, so we knew that, that, that we live in a paradise.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
We don't touch it.
